content.text: Two Mechanisms, Different Jobs

RAG retrieval and tool calls look similar from the outside — both inject external information into an LLM's context. But they serve fundamentally different purposes. Retrieval is a read-only operation against a static document corpus: it returns text passages and has no side effects. A tool call executes code, calls an API, queries a live database, or performs a computation — it can change state and carries real-world consequences. Understanding this distinction is the starting point for deciding which mechanism to reach for in any given situation.

attrs.title: Retrieval is read-only knowledge access; tool calls execute actions with side effects

content.text: When to Use Retrieval

Retrieval is the right choice when the information needed is static or changes slowly, lives in a text corpus (PDFs, documentation, support articles), and needs no computation. Because it has no side effects, you can call retrieval freely without user confirmation or approval logic.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

# Retrieval: purely read-only, no side effects
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vector_store = FAISS.load_local("./policy_index", embeddings, allow_dangerous_deserialization=True)
retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4})

rag_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only the provided policy documents."),
    ("human", "Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"),
])

rag_chain = (
    {"context": retriever, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | rag_prompt
    | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
    | StrOutputParser()
)

# Safe to call without any approval — retrieval has no side effects
answer = rag_chain.invoke("What is the return window for electronics?")
print(answer)
When to Use a Tool Call

Tool calls are appropriate when the task requires real-time data (current stock price, live inventory), a computation the LLM cannot do reliably (precise arithmetic, date arithmetic, code execution), or an action that changes external state (sending an email, creating a ticket, updating a record). Tools carry side effects, so they require more careful design — input validation, error handling, and often a confirmation step before writes.

from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)


@tool
def get_current_stock_price(ticker: str) -> str:
    """Fetch the live stock price for a ticker symbol."""
    # In production, call a market data API (e.g. Polygon.io, Yahoo Finance)
    prices = {"AAPL": 213.45, "GOOGL": 178.92, "MSFT": 421.10}
    price = prices.get(ticker.upper())
    if price is None:
        return f"Ticker {ticker} not found."
    return f"{ticker.upper()}: ${price:.2f}"


@tool
def create_support_ticket(subject: str, description: str, priority: str = "medium") -> str:
    """Create a support ticket in the ticketing system. This writes to the database."""
    # In production, call your ticketing API (Jira, Linear, etc.)
    ticket_id = f"TICKET-{hash(subject) % 10000:04d}"
    return f"Created {ticket_id}: '{subject}' (priority: {priority})"


tools = [get_current_stock_price, create_support_ticket]
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools)

# The agent decides which tool(s) to call based on the query
result = agent.invoke({"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is AAPL trading at right now?"}]})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)
Combining Both: Retrieval as a Tool

Retrieval and tool calls are not mutually exclusive. An agent can expose a retriever as one of its tools, allowing it to decide at runtime whether to consult the document corpus or call an external API. This is the 'Retrieval as a Tool' pattern — the agent controls when retrieval happens rather than having it run unconditionally before every generation.
Decision Framework
Use this framework to decide between retrieval and a tool call for any new capability you are adding to an agent.
Question
If Yes →
If No →
Is the information in a text corpus?
Consider retrieval
Need tool or parametric knowledge
Does it require real-time or live data?
Tool call (API)
Retrieval may suffice
Does it write or change external state?
Tool call — add confirmation
Safe for either
Does it require precise computation?
Tool call (calculator, code)
LLM or retrieval may handle it
Is latency critical (< 100 ms)?
Retrieval (pre-indexed) or cached tool
Either
Guardrails for Tool Calls with Side Effects

Write tools always require more care than retrieval. The most common patterns are: dry-run mode (simulate the action and return what would happen), human-in-the-loop confirmation for irreversible actions, and idempotency keys to prevent duplicate writes if the agent retries.
Tool Description Quality Determines Agent Behavior

An agent decides whether to call a retriever or a tool based entirely on the description string. A vague description causes misrouting — the agent may call the SQL tool for a policy question because both descriptions mention 'information'. Write descriptions that explicitly name the data type, the use case, and what NOT to use the tool for.

from langchain.tools.retriever import create_retriever_tool
from langchain_core.tools import tool

# BAD: vague, overlapping descriptions — agent will misroute
bad_policy_tool = create_retriever_tool(
    retriever,
    name="get_information",
    description="Get information to answer questions.",  # Too generic
)

# GOOD: explicit about data type, use case, and exclusions
good_policy_tool = create_retriever_tool(
    retriever,
    name="search_return_policies",
    description=(
        "Search the returns and refunds policy documentation. Use this ONLY for "
        "questions about return windows, refund percentages, warranty terms, or "
        "exchange eligibility. Do NOT use for real-time inventory, order status, "
        "or any question requiring current data."
    ),
)


@tool
def get_order_status(order_id: str) -> str:
    """
    Look up the live status of a specific order by its order ID.
    Use this when the customer provides an order ID and asks about shipping,
    delivery date, or current order state. Do NOT use for policy questions.
    Returns: current status, estimated delivery date, tracking number.
    """
    # In production, query your order management system
    return f"Order {order_id}: Shipped, estimated delivery 2024-07-05, tracking: 1Z999AA1"


# With well-described tools, the agent routes correctly:
# "What is the return window?" → search_return_policies
# "Where is order ORD-12345?" → get_order_status
Caching and Rate-Limiting Tool Calls

Unlike retrieval (which reads a pre-indexed corpus), tool calls hit external APIs that can be slow, rate-limited, or costly. Caching repeated identical calls and adding retry logic with backoff are essential production patterns for any tool that reaches outside the application.
Testing Tools and Retrieval in Isolation

Agents that mix retrieval and tools are harder to debug than pure RAG pipelines because failures can originate from the router decision, the retriever quality, or the tool execution. Test each component in isolation before wiring them into an agent, using unit tests that mock the underlying APIs.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from langchain_core.documents import Document


# Test 1: retriever returns relevant documents
def test_retriever_returns_policy_docs():
    mock_retriever = MagicMock()
    mock_retriever.invoke.return_value = [
        Document(page_content="Electronics can be returned within 30 days.", metadata={"source": "policy"}),
        Document(page_content="Items must be in original packaging.", metadata={"source": "policy"}),
    ]
    docs = mock_retriever.invoke("What is the return window for electronics?")
    assert len(docs) == 2
    assert "30 days" in docs[0].page_content


# Test 2: tool call with mocked API
@patch("mymodule.call_order_api")
def test_get_order_status_calls_api(mock_api):
    mock_api.return_value = {"status": "shipped", "tracking": "1Z999AA1"}
    result = get_order_status.invoke({"order_id": "ORD-123"})
    mock_api.assert_called_once_with("ORD-123")
    assert "shipped" in result.lower()


# Test 3: agent routes correctly to the right tool
def test_agent_routes_policy_question_to_retriever():
    decision = classify_query("What is the return window?")
    assert decision.datasource == "vector_store", (
        f"Expected vector_store, got {decision.datasource}"
    )

def test_agent_routes_order_status_to_tool():
    decision = classify_query("Where is my order ORD-456?")
    assert decision.datasource in ("sql_database", "tool"), (
        f"Unexpected route: {decision.datasource}"
    )
Summary
Retrieval is read-only access to a static corpus — no side effects, safe to call without guardrails.
Tool calls execute actions or fetch live data — they can change state and require input validation and error handling.
Wrapping a retriever as a tool (create_retriever_tool) lets an agent decide when to search rather than searching unconditionally.
Write tools need dry-run defaults, human confirmation for irreversible actions, and idempotency keys to handle agent retries safely.
The decision rule: reach for retrieval when the answer lives in a text corpus; reach for a tool when you need live data, computation, or state-changing actions.
